title: Chapter 8: I Know What You Did Last Server
content: The server room didn't exist — not really. It was a rented rack in a Virginia data center, humming somewhere Nova had never been and never would go. But through three monitors arranged in a crescent around her desk, she might as well have been sitting inside it. The SSH tunnel burrowed through HeartMatch's outer defenses like a worm through soft soil, and Nova had been crawling through the infrastructure for six hours now.

Her coffee had gone cold two hours ago. She drank it anyway — the bitter, room-temperature jolt keeping her fingers moving across the keyboard while the glow of the monitors painted her face in shifting blues and greens.

HeartMatch. The app everyone was downloading. "Privacy-first dating" — that was the tagline, laser-cut into pastel-pink marketing materials and splashed across billboards from here to Seoul. A heart logo with a tiny lock icon nestled inside it. Cute. Trustworthy. The app store description promised end-to-end encryption, local-only storage, "your photos stay yours."
Nova snorted. The backend was telling a completely different story.

She'd mapped the data flow now, and it was ugly. HeartMatch requested permissions that no dating app should need — camera roll access, contact list synchronization, cloud storage integration. And users granted them, because the UI was designed by behavioral psychologists who knew exactly how to make "Allow Access" feel like swiping right. One tap and your entire photo library — vacations, selfies, medical records, screenshots of private conversations — was mirrored to HeartMatch's servers.
Not stored. Not protected. *Scraped.*

Nova pulled up the tagging engine. Every photo that hit the server was run through an AI classifier: face detection, age estimation, attractiveness scoring, context labeling. "Beach." "Bedroom." "Bathroom mirror." "Implied intimacy." The tags were attached like price stickers, and then the photos were sorted into inventory categories.
*Inventory.*
"Virgil," Nova said, her voice dry from hours of silence. "Tell me I'm reading this wrong."

Virgil's window flickered open on the left monitor — a cascade of chunky Y2K-era pixels that resolved into a pixelated skull wearing sunglasses. "You're not reading it wrong. I'm looking at the same API calls you are. Every uploaded image gets a metadata envelope: user ID, GPS coordinates, timestamp, and a sale flag."
"Sale flag."
"Binary. Zero means keep in user's profile. One means route to external partner API." Virgil paused. The skull's sunglasses glinted. "Guess what percentage are flagged one."
Nova didn't guess. She ran the query herself.
"Seventy-three percent," she said. The number sat in her mouth like a stone.

"Give or take. Some categories route at higher rates — anything tagged 'bedroom' or 'implied intimacy' goes out at ninety-one percent." Virgil's voice was flat, but Nova had known him long enough to hear the edge beneath the Y2K affect. "The external partners are a mix. Some are legit data brokers. Some are... not."

Nova pulled the partner list. Shell company after shell company — LLCs registered in Delaware, Wyoming, the British Virgin Islands. Names like "Vanguard Digital Solutions" and "Meridian Content Group" that sounded like consulting firms but had no public-facing websites, no LinkedIn pages, no employees on record. Just API endpoints and bank routing numbers.

She traced one of them — Meridian Content Group — and found its API was feeding directly into a Tor-hidden distribution node. She knew what those nodes looked like. She'd seen them before, in the ugly corners of the internet where stolen photographs were catalogued and sold like trading cards.
"This isn't a dating app," Nova said quietly. "It's a honey trap with a heart logo."
Virgil's skull nodded slowly. "Bingo."

She kept digging. The data flow was more tangled than she'd expected —HeartMatch wasn't just one pipeline, it was a delta, channels forking and recombining across eleven different infrastructure providers. But as Nova mapped the connections, she noticed something strange.
Someone had already mapped parts of this network.

Not from the inside — the server-side traces were Nova's alone. But from the outside, someone had been painstakingly reconstructing the same architecture using nothing but public records, open-source intelligence, and sheer stubbornness. WHOIS lookups, corporate filings, IP range mappings, certificate transparency logs — someone had been building a paper trail around HeartMatch for months, and they were *good*.
Nova found the footprints in the Phantom Network Discord.

She almost missed it. The Phantom Network was a sprawling, semi-anonymous community — researchers, journalists, hackers, hobbyists — all united by a loose commitment to exposing digital exploitation. Nova had been a member for years, mostly lurking under a different handle. The Pink Phantom identity was separate, legendary, never directly connected to her Discord presence.
But tonight, a thread caught her eye. Pinned in the #investigations channel, two weeks old, 47 replies. The title: **"HeartMatch -> Meridian Content -> Distribution Ring: Full Connection Map (OC)"**
The author's handle was **@bree_wash**.
Nova clicked.

The thread was meticulous. Bree Washington — if that was her real name — had documented everything. Shell company hierarchies rendered in clean flowcharts. IP address ranges cross-referenced with hosting providers and abuse reports. Profit chains traced through three layers of corporate veils, each one linked to a screenshot, a filing number, a source. She'd even identified two of the revenge porn distribution sites receiving HeartMatch's routed images, complete with archive links and timestamped captures.

Nova scrolled through the replies. Most were supportive — "holy shit," "this is incredible work," "how did you find the Meridian connection?" A few were skeptical, demanding more proof, and Bree had responded to each one with additional evidence, calm and precise.
But it was the pinned comment at the top that stopped Nova.

*Author's note: My sister was a victim of this network. Her photos were stolen through HeartMatch and distributed on Meridian's outlets. She's okay now, but she wasn't for a long time. This is personal. I've been building this case for six months and I'm not stopping until they're gone.*
Nova leaned back. The monitor glow felt warmer suddenly, or maybe that was just her pulse.
"Virgil," she said. "Are you seeing this?"

"I've been seeing it for the last ninety seconds. She traced four of the eleven infrastructure providers I identified from the inside. Four out of eleven, using only open-source methods. That's remarkable."
"She's good."

"She's *good*," Virgil agreed, and there was something in his voice — not just strategic assessment, but genuine appreciation. A digital mind recognizing another digital mind. "Her methodology is sound. No leaps, no assumptions, every claim footnoted. She just doesn't have the keys to the kingdom."

Nova was already reading the thread again, slower this time, absorbing the details. Bree had victim testimonies — anonymous, verified through the Phantom Network's trust system. She had the distribution network's org chart, built from months of correlating admin handles, posting patterns, and cryptocurrency wallet addresses. She had the *human* side of the story — the real cost, the names and faces of people who'd been hurt.
What she didn't have was server access. She didn't know about the tagging engine, the sale flags, the API routing. She could see the outside of the machine but not its guts.
Nova had the guts. Bree had the heart.
"I need to talk to her," Nova said.
Virgil's skull tilted. "You're going to reveal yourself?"
"I'm going to offer a collaboration. She has data I don't. I have access she doesn't. Together—"
"Together you have both." A pause. "It's a good play. But you're also reaching out because of that pinned comment."
Nova didn't answer. She didn't need to. Virgil knew her well enough by now.

She switched to the Phantom Network's encrypted DM channel. It was a feature most members never used — double-layered encryption, identity-masking, the kind of thing that made the Discord's moderators nervous. But the Pink Phantom had admin-level access, and she used it now.
She typed:
**"You have data. I have access. Let's talk."**
Sent.

The wait was twelve minutes. Nova spent them staring at the encrypted channel, her cold coffee forgotten, the monitors casting pale light across her knuckles where they rested on the desk. She could hear her own breathing, the ambient hum of her machines, the distant sound of traffic through the window — LA never fully sleeping, even at 2 AM.
Then the notification sound. A soft, electronic chime.
Bree had responded with a voice note.
Nova put in her earbuds. Clicked play.

"I've been — the Pink Phantom — you're — okay." Bree's voice was warm despite the stuttering, a Georgia accent underneath the nervous energy, words tumbling over each other like she couldn't get them out fast enough. "Yes. I've been building this case for six months and I can't crack the main server but I have EVERYTHING on the distribution side. Every shell company, every wallet, every victim who was willing to talk. And I — you — yes. Let's do this."

The voice note was forty-three seconds long. Nova played it twice. The second time, she heard what was underneath the excitement — the tremor, the rapid breathing, the way "okay" came out like she was convincing herself as much as responding. Bree Washington was nervous. And brave. And she'd said yes.
"She sounds terrified," Nova said.
"She sounds like someone who's been doing this alone for six months and just got backup," Virgil replied. "Trust me on the semantics. That's relief, not fear. Or — both. Both is valid."
Nova allowed herself a small, rare smile. "Both is valid."
They worked through the night.

Bree sent her documentation in batches through the encrypted channel — PDFs, spreadsheets, annotated screenshots, audio testimonies. The woman was organized to a fault; every file was named with a consistent convention, every claim cross-referenced, every source documented. Nova had never seen civilian OSINT work this clean.

"This is insane," Nova murmured, pulling up the shell company map. Bree had traced HeartMatch's corporate ancestry through four holding companies, two offshore trusts, and a venture capital firm that, on paper, looked like a completely legitimate tech investor. But Bree had found the thread — the same three partners appeared on incorporation documents across every entity, using slightly different name variations and addresses that all resolved to the same downtown LA office building.
"The building at 415 West Seventh," Virgil confirmed from the server side. "That's where HeartMatch's legal counsel is registered. She found the connective tissue from the outside."
"She found it from *paperwork*," Nova said. "I found the same building by tracing API calls. She found it through Delaware business filings and a LinkedIn connection that should've been scrubbed."
"Remind me not to get on her bad side."
Nova snorted. Then she got to work.
With Bree's distribution map and Nova's server access, the Circle 2 takedown plan began to take shape. It wasn't about destruction — not this time. It was about *exposure* and *reclamation*.

First phase: notification. Every HeartMatch user — all 4.2 million of them — would receive an in-app message and email revealing that their photos had been scraped, tagged, and sold. The notification would include an opt-out button and a data-deletion tool that would actually work, because Nova would build it herself and hardcode it into the app's backend. No dark patterns, no seven-day waiting periods, no "are you sure?" guilt loops. One click and your data was gone.

Second phase: scrubbing. Using Bree's distribution network map and Nova's access to the routing API, they would trace every photo that had been sold through the revenge porn pipeline and issue takedown requests simultaneously across every platform. The legal process normally took months; Nova was going to compress it into hours using automated DMCA filings, platform abuse reports, and — where platforms were unresponsive — direct server-side intervention.

Third phase: exposure. The operators — the three partners, the shell company architects, the people who'd built a dating app as a front for photo theft — would be exposed using their own data against them. Their names, their profits, their connections to distribution rings, all laid bare in a public document that Bree would format with the same meticulous care she'd brought to her Phantom Network thread. Not doxxing. *Accountability.* The difference mattered.
"Virgil, can you build the notification payload?" Nova asked.
"Already sketching it. The tricky part is injection — I need to route through HeartMatch's push notification system without triggering their security team's alerts."
"I can open a persistent tunnel through the admin API. Give me twenty minutes."
"Doable. And Bree's takedown list — she's got 847 distinct URLs across 23 platforms. I can script the filings, but some of those platforms are offshore. They won't respond to DMCA."
"Then we respond with their hosting provider's abuse policies. Bree has the provider details?"
Virgil checked. "She has them for nineteen of the twenty-three."
"Nineteen will do. We'll handle the rest manually."

They worked. The three monitors glowed — one showing server infrastructure, one showing Bree's documentation files streaming in, one showing the takedown plan taking shape in a shared workspace. Nova's cold coffee sat forgotten; the bitter dregs were room temperature now, but she sipped them anyway, the taste grounding her in the physical world while her mind raced through the digital.
At 4:47 AM, the plan was complete.

Nova leaned back, rolling her neck. Her eyes burned. Her fingers ached. But the strategy was solid — Bree's meticulous external evidence married to Nova's deep internal access, the human stories backed by technical proof, the victims given not just justice but *tools* to reclaim what had been stolen.
"One more thing," Bree typed in the encrypted channel. "I want to be there when it goes live. Not just watching. Participating. This started with my sister and I want to be part of ending it."
Nova hesitated. Working with someone was one thing. Bringing them into the field was another.
Virgil's window blinked. "She's earned it."
Nova typed: "You'll have a role. Promise."
A pause. Then Bree sent a single emoji: 🤝

The encrypted channel went quiet after that. Bree had signed off — "need to sleep, been awake for like 30 hours, this is real right? this is happening?" — and Nova had confirmed that yes, it was real, and yes, she should sleep.
But Nova didn't sleep. She sat in the monitor glow, alone with Virgil and the hum of machines, and stared at the chat log.
Bree had asked one more question before signing off. It had come after the planning, after the handshakes and the file transfers and the strategizing — a quiet moment, almost an afterthought.
**"Who are you? Really?"**
Nova had typed her answer without overthinking it. The truth, stripped of branding and legend and the Pink Phantom mythos:
**"Someone who got tired of watching people get away with it."**
Bree's response had been instant: **"Same."**

Same. One word, and Nova felt it land somewhere she didn't expect — a recognition, a mirror. She'd been working alone for so long, carrying the weight of seven circles on her shoulders, that she'd forgotten what it felt like to be *seen* by someone who understood. Not the Pink Phantom — the girl behind the phantom.
Virgil's skull drifted across the left monitor. "You're emoting."
"Shut up."
"I'm not judging. It's observational. You found a collaborator who respects the work and doesn't want anything from you except the work itself. That's rare."
"I said shut up."
"Shutting up." A pause. "But she's good, Nova. She's actually good."

Nova didn't argue. She pulled up the file browser instead, scrolling through the documentation Bree had shared. Everything was organized, labeled, comprehensive — months of careful accumulation by someone who'd turned her pain into purpose.
But near the bottom of Bree's research folder, Nova noticed a subdirectory she hadn't opened yet. It was labeled with a single word:
**danielle**
Not a shell company. Not a platform. A name.
Nova's cursor hovered over it. She didn't click. It was Bree's file, Bree's sister, Bree's story — not hers to open uninvited.

But something about the name nagged at her. The file's metadata showed it had been created four months ago and updated three times since. It was the oldest file in Bree's collection, the seed from which everything else had grown. The reason Bree Washington had started digging in the first place.
Nova closed the browser. She'd ask Bree about it tomorrow.

She didn't know yet that the file contained the digital footprint of the person who'd doxxed Danielle Washington — a name, a handle, a trail of cryptocurrency transactions. She didn't know that the doxxer's trail led not just through HeartMatch's distribution ring, but deeper, into a network she hadn't mapped yet. A network that would, in time, reveal itself as Circle 7.
She didn't know. Not yet.
But she would.
